2006-12-01.title: Thailand: Plan to Override Patent for AIDS Drug
2006-12-01.abstract: Thailand announced that it would override a patent held by the pharmaceutical company Merck for a medicine to treat AIDS, efavirenz. The country’s own public drug manufacturer is expected to make a generic version for about $267 a patient per year, compared with Merck’s price of $466. Thailand and Brazil have hesitated to override drug patents, as international trade rules allow, fearing trade retaliation from the United States. A recent World Bank study concluded that unless multinational drug companies slashed prices or Thailand allowed more domestic production of cheap knockoffs, Thailand’s AIDS treatment costs would quintuple by 2020 from the current $100 million a year as patients need costly patented medicines.

2006-12-01.title: Radioactive Trail Is Found in Case of Poisoned Spy
2006-12-01.abstract: British officials said radiation was detected in 12 locations around London, and that it was likely to be found in more.

2006-12-01.title: Idea of Rapid Withdrawal From Iraq Seems to Fade
2006-12-01.abstract: News analysis: idea of rapid American troop withdrawal from Iraq is fast receding as viable option despite Democrats' victory in election viewed as referendum on war; most talked-about alternatives include renewed efforts to prepare Iraqi forces while preparing to pull American combat brigades back to their bases, or back home, sometime next year; but Pres Bush, who continues to insist that American troops must stay, has not been one to back down; he has staked his presidency on remaking Iraq; every day, chances of that seem more remote (M)

2006-12-01.title: Cardinal Faults China for Installing Bishop Without Approval
2006-12-01.abstract: Cardinal Joseph Zen accused China of having threatened and kidnapped clergy to make them participate in the ritual.

2006-12-01.title: Clinton Helps Broker Deal for Medicine to Treat AIDS
2006-12-01.abstract: Former President Bill Clinton announced that his foundation had negotiated deeply reduced prices for 19 AIDS drugs to treat children.

2006-12-02.title: P.L.O. Panel Is Convened Amid Standoff With Hamas
2006-12-02.abstract: Palestinian Authority pres Mahmoud Abbas convenes Palestine Liberation Organization's top decision-making group amid growing signs of possible political showdown with Hamas, faction that currently leads Palestinian government (M))

2006-12-02.title: World Briefing | Europe: The Netherlands: Reporters Freed After Protests
2006-12-02.abstract: Dutch reporters Bart Mos and Joost de Haas of daily newspaper De Telegraf are released after spending three days in jail for refusing to disclose their sources; they were called as witnesses in trial of former secret agent charged with leaking information about organized crime gang (S)

2006-12-02.title: Amid Catcalls, Mexico’s President Is Sworn In
2006-12-02.abstract: Felipe Calderón took the oath of office to become Mexico’s new president under chaotic and divisive conditions.

2006-12-02.title: Israeli Overflights Continue, U.N. Says
2006-12-02.abstract: Sec Gen Kofi Annan says cease-fire that ended fighting between Israel and Hezbollah fighters is holding, but that Israeli overflights in border area are occurring almost daily (S)

2006-12-02.title: Ukraine: Foreign Minister Ousted
2006-12-02.abstract: Parliament voted to dismiss the country’s Western-leaning foreign minister, Boris I. Tarasyuk, left, deepening a constitutional crisis as it was unclear whether the chamber had authority to do so. Under a new Constitution adopted this year, President Viktor A. Yushchenko has authority over foreign policy. But his political rival, Prime Minister Viktor F. Yanukovich, is head of the cabinet as leader of a Parliamentary coalition. That has left the position of foreign minister contested. Mr. Yushchenko’s chief of staff said the issue should be decided by the courts.

2006-12-03.title: Rumsfeld’s Memo of Options for Iraq War
2006-12-03.abstract: Text of classified memorandum Iraq -- Illustrative New Courses of Action sent by Defense Sec Donald H Rumsfeld to Pres Bush (M)

2006-12-03.title: In Turkey, Wisdom Awaits Those Who Call Help Line
2006-12-03.abstract: The Turkish Religious Affairs Directorate help line offers guidance, known as fatwas, when asked by Muslims.

2006-12-03.title: Rumsfeld Memo Proposed ‘Major Adjustment’ in Iraq
2006-12-03.abstract: Two days before he resigned as defense secretary, Donald H. Rumsfeld submitted a memo to the White House that acknowledged that the administration’s strategy in Iraq was not working.

2006-12-03.title: Russian Ex-Spy Lived in a World of Deceptions
2006-12-03.abstract: Alexander V. Litvinenko’s career began in the post-Soviet roller coaster and ended mysteriously when he died of radiation poisoning last week.

2006-12-03.title: Typhoon Death Toll Expected to Rise in Philippines
2006-12-03.abstract: Rescue workers began burying landslide victims in a mass grave today, and officials warned that the death toll of more than 400 was likely to rise.

2006-12-04.title: Bolton to Leave Post as U.S. Envoy to United Nations
2006-12-04.abstract: The administration was unwilling to make another end run around Congressional opponents to keep John R. Bolton.

2006-12-04.title: U.S. Marine Guilty in Philippine Rape Case
2006-12-04.abstract: The conviction ended an emotional year-long case that tested Philippine-American relations and revived calls for the abrogation of a controversial defense agreement.

2006-12-04.title: Britain Says Inquiry Into Poisoning of Russian Will Broaden
2006-12-04.abstract: Media reports said British counterterrorism officers were planning to fly to Moscow to interview witnesses in the case.

2006-12-04.title: Pinochet in Stable Condition After Heart Attack
2006-12-04.abstract: Gen Augusto Pinochet, former dictator of Chile, suffers serious heart attack, undergoes angioplasty and is in stable condition; Marco Antonio Pinochet, his son, says that his father has received last rites; Pres Michelle Bachelet says that Gen Pinochet will not be entitled to state funeral, given that he has been indicted for crimes (M)

2006-12-04.title: At Least 3 Die in Afghanistan as Bombing Incites Shooting
2006-12-04.abstract: Three were killed as British troops opened fire on civilians after a suicide bomber rammed a minibus into their military convoy.

2006-12-05.title: John Bolton to Leave U.N.
2006-12-05.abstract: United Nations Bureau Chief Warren Hoge discusses Ambassador John Bolton's departure from the United Nations.

2006-12-05.title: Israel Releases Lebanon Attack Video
2006-12-05.abstract: Greg Myre of The Times analyzes a multimedia report by the Israeli Defense Force that purports to show how Hezbollah militias used civilians as cover for their missile attacks last summer.

2006-12-05.title: World Briefing | Middle East: Egypt: American Held In Terror Cell Case
2006-12-05.abstract: Egyptian security forces charge group of foreigners, including one American, with trying to form secret Islamic terrorist cell that would recruit fighters to go to Iraq (S)

2006-12-05.title: U.S. Troops in Iraq Shifting to Advisory Roles
2006-12-05.abstract: US commanders in Iraq are shifting thousands of combat troops into advisory positions with Iraqi Army and police units, especially in Baghdad, in effort to bring sectarian violence under control; commanders say they believe that major influx of US advisers will help Iraqi units take security lead; commanders acknowledge risks in shifting troops, including risk that Iraqi-led operations may be less effective and result in more casualties; photo (M)

2006-12-05.title: Chile: Pinochet Said to Be Recovering From Surgery
2006-12-05.abstract: Military hospital officials in Santiago said the former dictator Augusto Pinochet, 91, had improved steadily and would probably recover well from angioplasty and a second operation. He was hospitalized Sunday morning after suffering an acute heart attack and lung edema. Although his condition is still considered critical, he has progressed “so positively” that there is much less risk of complications, said Dr. Juan Ignacio Vergara, who leads the medical team.

2006-12-06.title: Afghanistan's Stone Buddhas
2006-12-06.abstract: Carlotta Gall reports from Afghanistan on efforts to restore the vast stone buddhas in Bamiyan that were dynamited by the Taliban in 2001.

2006-12-06.title: Theologians Battle Female Circumcision
2006-12-06.abstract: In Cairo, a small revolution has been launched: A conference of high-ranking Muslim theologians has agreed that the practice of female genital mutilation is irreconcilable with Islam. The painful and often deadly practice of female circumcision affects millions of women in Africa.      Fatima's scream is as blood-curdling as it is heart-wrenching. The little girl, who looks to be about eight years old, screams in a panic, initially in fear and then because she is unable to bear the pain she is experiencing. She is lying on the floor of a dirty hut somewhere in the Ethiopian desert. Her body is contorted with pain as she screams, cries and finally lies there whimpering. Her new, green floral dress is soaked in blood.

2006-12-06.title: As War Ravages Baghdad, City’s Ambulance Workers Must Pick Up the Pieces
2006-12-06.abstract: As the city has disintegrated around them, ambulance workers are often overwhelmed, and have increasingly become targets themselves.

2006-12-06.title: This Time Around, a Shorter Confirmation Hearing and Praise for Candor
2006-12-06.abstract: Senate Armed Services Committee hearing for Robert M Gates, Pres Bush's nominee as defense secretary, is marked by warm welcome from both parties; members praise his candor, forthrightness and pledges of bipartisanship and dialogue; brief excerpts; photo (M)

2006-12-06.title: World Briefing | Europe: Britain: Pynchon Joins Defense In Plagiarism Case
2006-12-06.abstract: Letter appearing on London Daily Telegraph Web site from reclusive British author Thomas Pynchon defends fellow writer Ian McEwan in plagiarism scandal; McEwan is accused of copying phrases from book No Time for Romance by Lucilla Andrews for his 2001 best-selling novel Atonement (S)

2006-12-07.title: Cuba: Dissident Released
2006-12-07.abstract: The government released Héctor Palacios, left, leader of the banned All United opposition group, from prison for health reasons. Mr. Palacios was among 75 dissidents arrested in 2003 on charges of illegally cooperating with the United States government. It was the first such release since Cuba’s leader, Fidel Castro, fell ill in late July and ceded power to his brother, Raúl. Mr. Palacios, 65, said he felt physically “destroyed” but remained “morally strong” in his opposition to the Cuban government, The Associated Press reported. Opposition leaders wrote this week to Kofi Annan, secretary general of the United Nations, asking him to monitor a human rights demonstration they plan for Sunday.

2006-12-07.title: Suicide Bomber Strikes at American Firm in Southern Afghanistan
2006-12-07.abstract: Suicide bomber blows himself up outside offices of American firm in Kandahar, Afghanistan, killing two American security officers and nine Afghan members of staff, although there is some confusion about number of Afghan deaths (M)

2006-12-07.title: Son of Liberia’s Ex-Leader Charged in Miami Under Anti-Torture Law
2006-12-07.abstract: Son of former Liberian president Charles G Taylor, Charles McArthur Emmanuel, is charged with two counts of torture during interrogation of opposition figure in Monrovia; case is first in which federal authorities invoke anti-torture law which gives American courts jurisdiction over reported abuse overseas by American citizens; Emmanuel, also known as Charles Taylor Jr, was born in United States; elder Taylor is awaiting trial in The Hague on war crimes charges (M)

2006-12-07.title: Israeli Official Discusses Iran and His Controversial Agenda
2006-12-07.abstract: Avigdor Lieberman, Israel's minister for strategic affairs, is making his first visit to US in that capacity to meet with American officials, including Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice; will be discussing Iran, which he calls threat to Jewish people; supports US efforts to find a way to halt or contain Iran's nuclear program but has no faith in diplomatic efforts; Lieberman's most provocative plan calls for dividing Jews and Arabs into two homogeneous states, proposal his Arab critics often describe as racist; photo (M)

2006-12-07.title: Canada’s Police Commissioner Resigns Over Deportation Case
2006-12-07.abstract: Prime Min Stephen Harper of Canada announces resignation of commissioner of Royal Canadian Mounted Police, Giuliano Zaccardelli; he is first official to face penalties in case of Maher Arar, Canadian citizen deported to Syria, then jailed and tortured because he was suspected of being terrorist; commission of inquiry declared that Arar was innocent and was deported by American officials because of false information they received from Mounted Police; Zaccardelli has admitted that his initial testimony about how he handled Arar affair had been incorrect; pressure on him has been rising since release of court decision critical of force for trying to muzzle reporter covering Arar (M)

2006-12-08.title: With Foe’s Change of Heart, Iraq Watchdog Is Likely to Survive
2006-12-08.abstract: A key House Republican dropped his opposition against a federal agency that oversees billions in reconstruction money to Iraq.

2006-12-08.title: Catholic Clergy Attack French Telethon Over Stem Cell Aid
2006-12-08.abstract: Roman Catholic Church in France sullies reputation of wildly popular annual telethon run by country's Muscular Dystrophy Assn to raise money for medical research; at issue is telethon money donated for stem cell research, which some church officials claim is immoral; in France, where separation of church and state is unshakable pillar, these attacks are being met with sharp resistance by government officials and leaders of French medical establishment; even Pees Jacques Chirac enters fray, staunchly defending telethon and upholding 2004 bioethics law the permits stem cell research; photos (M)

2006-12-08.title: World Briefing | Americas: Canada: Parliament Upholds Law Allowing Same-Sex Marriage
2006-12-08.abstract: Canadian Parliament votes down motion by government of Prime Minister Stephen Harper to revisit 2005 law that allows same-sex marriages (s),

2006-12-08.title: Israeli Leader Rejects Link Between Iraq and Mideast Woes
2006-12-08.abstract: Prime Min Ehud Olmert of Israel says he disagrees with Iraq Study Group's claim of strong link between turmoil in Iraq and need to resolve conflicts between Israel and its Arab neighbors; he also objects to recommendation in report that urges talks involving Israel, Palestinians, Syria and Lebanon; Olmert reiterates his willingness to restart direct talks with Palestinians, with whom Israel has not had full-fledged negotiations in nearly six years, but not with Syria; photo (M)

2006-12-08.title: Uneasy Havens Await Those Who Flee Iraq
2006-12-08.abstract: Iraqis fleeing the chaos of their country now threaten the social and economic fabric of Jordan and Syria.

2006-12-09.title: World Briefing | Middle East: Iran: Palestinian Rejects Recognizing Israel
2006-12-09.abstract: Palestinian Prime Min Ismail Haniya tells supportive Iranian audience at Tehran University that Hamas-led government would never recognize Israel and would not respect agreements with it (S)

2006-12-09.title: Taiwan Leader's Party Ekes Out Win
2006-12-09.abstract: President Chen Shui-bian's Democratic Progressive Party eked out the narrowest victory in Taiwanese political history in crucial mayoral elections here on Saturday, but the opposition Nationalist Party quickly said it would seek a recount.     The Central Election Commission in Taipei announced that with all ballots counted, the Democratic Progressive Party candidate in Kaohsiung had won by 1,120 votes out of more than 760,000 cast, a victory margin of 14 hundredths of a percent. On an island with already deep political divisions, the narrow margin and the possibility of a court fight are likely to cause further political friction in the weeks ahead.

2006-12-09.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Chechnya School Siege Toll Up To 334
2006-12-09.abstract: Death toll from terrorist siege of middle school in Beslan, Chechnya, in 2004 rises to 334 when hostage, Yelena Avdonina, dies from internal bleeding (S)

2006-12-09.title: Federal Raid on Offices in Troubled Mexican State
2006-12-09.abstract: Federal agents raid state police headquarters in troubled state of Oaxaca, Mex, where law-enforcement authorities have failed to arrest anyone in killings of at least 11 antigovernment protesters; Bradley Roland Will, independent journalist from New York City, was among victims (M)

2006-12-09.title: Jeane Kirkpatrick, Reagan’s Forceful Envoy, Dies
2006-12-09.abstract: Jeane J. Kirkpatrick, a beacon of neoconservative thought, helped chart foreign policy from 1981 to 1985.

2006-12-10.title: Augusto Pinochet, 91, Ex-Dictator of Chile, Dies
2006-12-10.abstract: Gen. Augusto Pinochet Ugarte, who repressed and reshaped Chile for nearly two decades, became a  symbol of human rights abuse and corruption.

2006-12-10.title: Safety Errors Cited in Fire That Killed 45 in Moscow
2006-12-10.abstract: Russian officials say 45 women died in fire at Moscow Drug Treatment Hospital Nov 17 because they were trapped by locked exits and locked window grilles as toxic smoke suffocated them; officials suspect arson and are investigating further; no rescue operations were carried out by personnel; fire began in kitchen that had undergone repairs previous day (M)

2006-12-10.title: Shiites Rout Sunni Families in Mixed Area of Baghdad
2006-12-10.abstract: Armed Shiite militiamen stormed through a Sunni neighborhood, driving hundreds from their homes, an Iraqi Army officer said.

2006-12-10.title: Black-Market Weapon Prices Surge in Iraq Chaos
2006-12-10.abstract: Weapon prices are soaring in Iraq amid sectarian strife as more buyers push prices several times higher than those that existed at time of American-led invasion; rising prices encourage migration of army and police weapons from Iraqi state armories to black-market sales; American-issue weapons are also on sale in shops and bazaars, purchased by United States for Iraqi forces; photo; map (L)

2006-12-10.title: Nobel Peace Prize Winner Will Urge Banks to Lend to the Poor
2006-12-10.abstract: Nobel Peace Prize winner Muhammad Yunus will use his celebrity to urge bankers everywhere to make small business loans to poorest people they can find; is credited with inventing microfinance with his Grameen Bank 30 years ago in Bangladesh to help people, most of them women, to start or expand money-making enterprises; practice has put Bangladesh on path to halving number of people classified as extremely poor by 2015 (M)

2006-12-11.title: Dubai-Based Company Sells U.S. Port Businesses
2006-12-11.abstract: Terminal operations at six American seaports were sold today, closing a controversial chapter in American policy.

2006-12-11.title: In Tsunami’s Wake, Old Foes in War Now Wage Politics
2006-12-11.abstract: Tsunami that killed 170,000 people and left half million homeless in Indonesia's Aceh Province seems to have taken fire out of 29-year conflict between government and separatist rebels; hard-won peace agreement that made election possible appears to be holding; if process continues to move forward, armed insurgency group will perform rare feat of transforming itself into political party within government it opposed; photos (M)

2006-12-11.title: After an American Dies, the Case Against His Killers Is Mired in Mexican Justice
2006-12-11.abstract: The case of an American journalist’s death in Oaxaca reveals the sad clash of left-wing idealism with the murky realities of Mexican politics.

2006-12-11.title: Iraqi Faults U.S. Training Effort
2006-12-11.abstract: Iraq's president criticized the United States's effort to train security forces and its plans to increase advisers to the Iraqi Army. Page A12.

2006-12-11.title: Taliban and Allies Tighten Grip in North of Pakistan
2006-12-11.abstract: Taliban has created virtual mini-state in northern Pakistan, using recent peace deal with government to vastly expand training of suicide bombers and fortifying its alliance with Al Qaeda and foreign fighters; Pakistani militants are said to be openly flouting terms of Sept accord, under which they agreed to end cross-border help for Taliban insurgency that revived in Afghanistan with new force this year; foreign fighters are challenging government authority in area, and also wresting control from local tribes and spreading their influence to neighboring areas; tribes offered refuge to militants when they retreated to area in 2002 after American invasion of Afghanistan, but that welcome is waning in face of killings this year of more than 100 local leaders, government sympathizers or accused 'American spies'; links among various groups date to 1980's, when Arabs, Pakistanis and other Muslims joined Afghans in fight to drive Soviet Union out of Afghanistan; Pakistani, Afghan, American and NATO officials ponder what can be done to bring region under control; photos; maps (L)

2006-12-12.title: Pope Calls Energy Alternatives a Source of Peace
2006-12-12.abstract: In an annual message for peace, Benedict strongly emphasized a theme rarely taken up in his nearly two years as pope: what he called the “ecology of peace.”

2006-12-12.title: U.N. Reports on Inquiry Into Hariri Assassination
2006-12-12.abstract: The  inquiry into the assassination of the former Lebanese prime minister is in a “sensitive and complicated phase,” the head of the investigation said.

2006-12-12.title: Hospitalizations in Hamburg Tied to Radiation Trail in London
2006-12-12.abstract: Four people who had contact with Dmitri V Kovtun, business associate of former Russian agent Alexander V Litvinenko in Hamburg, Germany, are hospitalized there on suspicion they had been contaminated by polonium 210; Kovtun met with Litvinenko in London on day before Litvinenko became ill from radiation poisoning that killed him three weeks later; Kovtun spread wide trail of radiation during his brief stopover in Hamburg; his movements have become central to solving Litvenenko's death; he is reported to be in hospital in Moscow; photo (M)

2006-12-12.title: Algeria: U.S. Embassy Issues Warning After Attack
2006-12-12.abstract: The United States Embassy warned Americans to be on alert to possible attacks after a bombing on Sunday killed the driver of a bus carrying foreign oil workers and injured nine passengers. Four Britons and one American were among those wounded in the attack, the first in years on Western expatriates working in the country. Algerian authorities are still battling a small Islamist insurgency, remnants of the civil war that ravaged the country through the 1990s. Sunday’s attack, against employees of Brown & Root Condor, a joint venture of the Halliburton subsidiary KBR, the former Kellogg, Brown & Root, and Condor Engineering, an affiliate of Algeria’s state energy company, happened in the upscale Bouchaoui district west of Algiers.

2006-12-12.title: Iran Opens Conference on Holocaust
2006-12-12.abstract: The gathering included Holocaust deniers, discredited scholars and white supremacists under the guise of a “debate” on the era.

2006-12-13.title: Bush Consults With Pentagon on Iraq
2006-12-13.abstract: President Bush said today that United States troops would not leave Iraq “before the job is done” and the country is free and stable.

2006-12-13.title: Chinese Rights Lawyer Is Put on Trial
2006-12-13.abstract: An outspoken human rights lawyer faced charges of inciting subversion, but his lawyer and his family were prevented from attending the trial, his lawyer said.

2006-12-13.title: Saudis Say They Might Back Sunnis if U.S. Leaves Iraq
2006-12-13.abstract: Saudi Arabia’s warning reflects fears among America’s Sunni Arab allies about Iran’s rising influence in Iraq.

2006-12-13.title: Saudis Say They Might Back Sunnis if U.S. Leaves Iraq
2006-12-13.abstract: Saudi Arabia reportedly tells Bush administration it might provide financial backing to Iraqi Sunnis in war against Shiites if US pulls out troops; King Abdullah conveyed grim message during Vice Pres Cheney's visit; reportedly also opposed US-Iran talks and pushed for resumption of Israeli-Palestinian talks; warning reflects fears about Shiite Iran's rising influence; Saudis fear massacre of Sunnis, although they are also wary of supporting Sunni insurgents led by Al Qaeda extremists; Amb Prince Turki al-Faisal, who has told staff he is leaving Washington, dismissed consultant Nawaf Obaid over Washington Post article predicting that US pullout would bring 'massive Saudi intervention' and oil production increase to lower prices and destroy Iranian economy; that apparently represents Saudi government view; disarray reportedly over Turki resignation (M)

2006-12-13.title: Vatican City: Pope Warns of Energy and Arms Races
2006-12-13.abstract: Pope Benedict XVI challenged the world to find greater peace through a deeper respect for human rights, as he lamented terrorism, civilian deaths in the war in Lebanon and the spread of nuclear weapons. In his annual message for peace, Benedict worried about “the race for energy sources” and well as a “fear of a possible atomic catastrophe.” He also urged a greater respect for humanitarian law, saying that in Lebanon, the duty to protect, help and avoid involving the civilian population “was largely ignored.” He mentioned neither Hezbollah nor Israel by name. Even faced with what he called the “scourge of terrorism,” states should not react with unrestrained force if doing so would endanger civilians, he wrote, and called for “reflection on the ethical limits restricting the use of modern methods of guaranteeing internal security.”.

2006-12-14.title: Deep Roots of Denial for Iran’s True Believer
2006-12-14.abstract: Iran’s president, Mahmoud Ahmadinejad, actually seems to believe that the Holocaust is at best exaggerated and part of a conspiracy.

2006-12-14.title: Botswana: Bushmen Win Right to Go Home
2006-12-14.abstract: Botswana’s dwindling tribe of Bushmen won a crucial legal victory when the High Court ruled that the government had wrongly evicted the tribe from its diamond-rich ancestral lands in a stretch of Kalahari Desert that is now a state-protected game reserve larger than Switzerland. The judges ruled that the Bushmen legally occupied the land in 2002 when Botswana’s government forced them out. The Bushmen claimed they were driven out to allow diamond mining. Government officials said it was trying to protect the reserve’s animals from Bushmen hunters. The three-member judicial panel said the government had the right to cut off services, including water. But it acted illegally when it demolished tribal villages and trucked families of Bushmen to nearby settlements, the court said. About 2,000 Bushmen lived in the reserve when Botswana first began its forced relocations in 1997. It is not clear how many, if any, will return.

2006-12-14.title: World Briefing | Africa: Nigeria: $180 Million To Combat Malaria
2006-12-14.abstract: World Bank approves $180 million no-interest loan to Nigeria for bank's largest ever malaria-control project (S)

2006-12-14.title: Circumcision Halves H.I.V. Risk, U.S. Agency Finds
2006-12-14.abstract: National Institutes of Health says circumcision appears to reduce man's risk of contracting AIDS from heterosexual sex by half; NIH halts two clinical trials in Kenya and Uganda, saying that not offering circumcision to all men taking part would be unethical; AIDS experts hail finding but caution that circumcision only lessens chances that man will catch virus and should be used with other prevention methods; they say procedure is expensive and has serious risks if performed under unsanitary conditions, as often happens in rural Africa; two largest funds for fighting AIDS say they will consider paying for circumcisions in high-risk countries (M)

2006-12-14.title: Bush Won’t Be ‘Rushed’ on Iraq, but Says He’ll Press Maliki
2006-12-14.abstract: Pres Bush says he is 'not going to be rushed' into making decisions on new strategy in Iraq but intends to press Prime Min Nuri Kamal al-Maliki's government to reach out to Iraq's minority Sunni Muslim population; makes statement after meeting at Pentagon with top civilian and military leaders, including outgoing Defense Sec Donald H Rumsfeld and man who will replace him, Robert M Gates; Bush indicates he will rely heavily on Gates's advice in deciding on new strategy; senior administration official claims Bush decided to postpone national address on Iraq strategy when he realized that Gates needs time to get up to speed; Defense Dept officials say strategy review is likely to back plan being drafted by Gen George W Casey Jr that calls for shifting thousands of American combat troops into training teams responsible for advising Iraqi Army and police units; photo (M)

2006-12-15.title: Military Considers Sending as Many as 35,000 More U.S. Troops to Iraq, McCain Says
2006-12-15.abstract: Sen John McCain, leading Congressional delegation to Iraq, says American military commanders are considering adding as many as 10 more combat brigades--35,000 troops--to 'bring the situation under control' while Iraq's divided political leaders seek solutions to worsening bloodshed; McCain says that without troop increase, results will be defeat for America and for its Iraqi allies that would create terrorist haven that could be used as base for attacks in United States; but American commanders in Iraq remain divided on recommendations they will present to Pres Bush, with some saying that troop increase could lower violence in Baghdad but also encourage Iraq's feuding political leaders to delay tough decisions needed to stem slide toward anarchy; photo (M)

2006-12-15.title: Cuba’s Rap Vanguard Reaches Beyond the Party Line
2006-12-15.abstract: Alarmed by young people in baggy clothing and ill-aligned baseball caps, the government created the Cuban Rap Agency to bring rebellious rhymers into the fold.

2006-12-15.title: Bush Celebrates Early Victories in Campaign Against Malaria
2006-12-15.abstract: The Bushes hosted a forum at the National Geographic Society in Washington to raise the profile of the neglected scourge.

2006-12-15.title: Blair Defends Decision to Drop Inquiry into Defense Contractor
2006-12-15.abstract: The prime minister said today that a corruption probe into a deal between BAE Systems and Saudi Arabia would damage Britain if it continued.

2006-12-15.title: New U.N. Leader Is Sworn in and Promises to Rebuild Trust
2006-12-15.abstract: Ban Ki-moon pledged to rebuild faith in an organization that has been riven by disputes between rich and poor nations.

2006-12-16.title: World Briefing | Americas: Bolivia: East Marches For Autonomy
2006-12-16.abstract: Hundreds of thousands of protestors flood main avenues of Santa Cruz and other provincial capitals in eastern Bolivia to call for greater political autonomy from central government (S)

2006-12-16.title: Ties Cut With Iran Institute Over Holocaust
2006-12-16.abstract: Some of world's leading policy institutes announce that they are breaking off relations with group within Iranian Foreign Ministry that organized conference on Holocaust in Tehran featuring discredited academics and Holocaust deniers (M)

2006-12-16.title: Inuit Climate Change Petition Rejected
2006-12-16.abstract: The Inter-American Commission on Human Rights, an arm of the Organization of American States, has declined to rule on a complaint by native Arctic peoples that global warming caused by gases from the United States violates their right to sustain their traditional ways. The agency told the Inuit Circumpolar Conference, which represents 150,000 people in northern Alaska, Canada, Russia and Greenland, that there was insufficient evidence of harm. Inuit leaders said they would seek a hearing to present more evidence.

2006-12-16.title: In Raising the World’s I.Q., the Secret’s in the Salt
2006-12-16.abstract: Kazakhstan’s iodized salt campaign is a remarkable public health success for a still-developing nation.

2006-12-16.title: Canada: Arar Still on U.S. Watch List
2006-12-16.abstract: Maher Arar, the Canadian the United States sent in 2002 to Syria, where he was tortured for months, remains in a database of terrorist suspects maintained by the United States, despite a two-year Canadian investigation that found him to be innocent of any terrorist ties this year. David H. Wilkins, the American ambassador, acknowledged the continued listing of Mr. Arar on Canadian radio. In a later statement issued by the embassy, Mr. Wilkins said that the Canadian inquiry “was not privy to our information” and that the decision to include Mr. Arar on the list and to keep his name there were both “based on information from a variety of sources.” He said a lawsuit Mr. Arar has brought against the United States prevented him from providing further details.

2006-12-17.title: Blair, in Iraq, Offers Support for Leaders
2006-12-17.abstract: During a surprise visit, Prime Minister Tony Blair pledged his support for the Iraqi government and said British troops would remain “until the job is done.”

2006-12-17.title: Japan Rightists Fan Fury Over North Korea Abductions
2006-12-17.abstract: Japanese Prime Min Shinzo Abe makes special appearance at rally to call attention to Japanese citizens abducted by North Korea three decades ago and believed to still be held there; North Korean leader Kim Jong Il admitted four years ago that abductions occurred and has returned five survivors; issue is highly emotional and has contributed to silencing of Japan's moderates; Abe rose from obscurity to prime minister by championing cause; abduction issue is so delicate that media does not report on right-wing groups behind movement; photos (M)

2006-12-17.title: Poisoned Spy’s Wife Says He Feared Kremlin’s Long Reach
2006-12-17.abstract: Alexander Litvinenko's widow Marina says he began to worry about safety of Russian exiles like himself in July, when Russian Parliament approved law legalizing strikes beyond Russia's borders against those government considers to be extremists or terrorists; says husband still felt protected by asylum in Britain; photo (M)

2006-12-17.title: Overstretched U.S. Forces Are ‘Losing’ in Iraq, Powell Says
2006-12-17.abstract: The former secretary of state said today that a temporary increase in troop levels in Iraq probably would not help.

2006-12-17.title: Iraqi Chief Calls Forum to Press for National Reunification; Major Groups Are Absent
2006-12-17.abstract: Iraq’s prime minister, Nuri Kamal al-Maliki, convened leaders from various communities across the country for talks about how to stem the bloodshed.

2006-12-18.title: Nigerian Party Selects Its Presidential Candidate
2006-12-18.abstract: Nigeria’s governing party selected a reclusive northern governor to be its candidate in next year’s presidential election.

2006-12-18.title: Names of the Dead
2006-12-18.abstract: Defense Department confirms deaths of five American service members in Iraq, bringing total to 2,933 (S)

2006-12-18.title: Powell Doubts Need to Raise Troop Levels
2006-12-18.abstract: The former secretary of state said the Army was “about broken” and that he saw nothing to justify an increase in troops in Iraq.

2006-12-18.title: Liberal Opposition Activists Garner Attention
2006-12-18.abstract: A summary of the top stories in Russian newspapers on Monday.

2006-12-18.title: After Deadly Clashes in Gaza, Leaders Appeal for Calm
2006-12-18.abstract: Palestinian Authority Pres Mahmoud Abbas and acting speaker of Palestinian parliament Ahmed Baher of Hamas appeal separately to militants of all stripes to lay down their weapons after day of rallies and violent clashes in Gaza Strip; Hamas and Fatah spokesmen say armed groups have agreed on cease-fire, but that might prove as shaky as others have before it; Abbas, leader of Fatah, says he is fed up with failure of Hamas to negotiate seriously on new unity government and he orders early presidential and parliamentary elections; Hamas rejects early elections as illegal, saying they would be blatant effort to overturn their majority, won in free elections 11 months ago; they accuse Abbas of following American and Israeli agenda; photo (M)

2006-12-19.title: Man Arrested in British Serial Stranglings
2006-12-19.abstract: Tom Stephens, supermarket worker, is arrested on suspicion of serial killings of five prostitutes near small town of Trimley St Martin in eastern England; police immediately begin painstaking search of his home for evidence and take away his car on flatbed truck; it is first arrest since women's bodies were found; Stephens had earlier granted interview in tabloid The Sunday Mirror in which he described himself as frequenter of prostitutes with close personal knowledge of five dead women; case has riveted nation, evoking comparisons with unsolved murders of Jack the Ripper; Stephens has not been immediately charged with crimes; photos (M)

2006-12-19.title: In Abbas, Western Hopes Hang on Thin Reed
2006-12-19.abstract: Mahmoud Abbas, the moderate Palestinian Authority president, has little power to carry out his decrees or his will.

2006-12-19.title: Romanian Leader Condemns Communist Rule
2006-12-19.abstract: The move by Traian Basescu was the first time a Romanian head of state had officially denounced the Soviet-era system.

2006-12-19.title: Graphic Images of Deaths Shown at Hussein Trial
2006-12-19.abstract: Horrific images of villagers being slaughtered in what prosecutors said was a chemical attack directed at ethnic Kurds were shown at the trial today.

2006-12-19.title: British Police Arrest 2nd Suspect in Serial Killings
2006-12-19.abstract: A police spokesman, speaking on condition of anonymity under police rules, described the man’s arrest as “significant.”

2006-12-20.title: Lawyer Predicts Bloodshed if Hussein Is Executed
2006-12-20.abstract: Former U.S. Attorney General Ramsey Clark, a member of the ex-dictator’s defense team, also called the trial unfair.

2006-12-20.title: World Briefing | Asia: North Korea Nuclear Talks Head Into Third Day
2006-12-20.abstract: Christopher R Hill, chief American negotiator on North Korea's nuclear program, says North Korea's nuclear test in October has brought China and United States closer together at six-nation talks under way in Beijing (S)

2006-12-20.title: U.S. Hands Najaf Province Over to Iraqi Forces
2006-12-20.abstract: It is the third province in Iraq to have power transferred, the previous two having been under British control.

2006-12-20.title: Captain to Face Charges in Marines’ Killings of Iraqis, Lawyer Says
2006-12-20.abstract: Capt Lucas McConnell, officer responsible for marines who killed two dozen Iraqis, including several women and children, in village of Haditha in 2005, will be charged with dereliction of duty or similar crime (M)

2006-12-20.title: World Briefing | Asia: Afghanistan: Government Opens Door To Poppy Spraying
2006-12-20.abstract: Afghanistan government says it will use ground spraying to reduce poppy crop but does not say where or when; 2006 brought record harvest with 49 percent increase over year before (S)

2006-12-21.title: Abdul-Amir al-Jamri, 67, Bahrain Shiite Cleric, Dies
2006-12-21.abstract: Sheik Abdul-Amir al-Jamri, Bahrain's most prominent Shiite Muslim cleric, dieas at age 67 (M)

2006-12-21.title: Bush Asserts That Victory in Iraq Is Still ‘Achievable’
2006-12-21.abstract: Pres Bush warns Americans that war in Iraq will require 'difficult choices and additional sacrifices' in coming year and firmly rejects notion that war cannot be won, year-end news conference; confirms plans to propose an increase in permament size of both Army and Marines; expresses optimism that he can work with Democrats in 2007; photo (M)

2006-12-21.title: Saparmurat Niyazov, Turkmen Leader, Dies at 66
2006-12-21.abstract: The sudden death raised questions about succession and stability for an essential supplier of energy to Europe.

2006-12-21.title: Leaving NATO, Marine General Still Seeks Troops for Afghanistan
2006-12-21.abstract: NATO’s 26 members and 11 partners have about 32,000 troops in Afghanistan, including 11,000 Americans.

2006-12-21.title: Russia: Kremlin Sees More Spies
2006-12-21.abstract: President Vladimir V. Putin said foreign spies in Russia had been “showing heightened interest in classified economic information,” the latest Kremlin expression of its worry that it has been under a growing threat from outside intelligence services. Mr. Putin’s remarks, made on Security Services Day, followed public comments Tuesday by Nikolai P. Patrushev, the head of the domestic intelligence agency, who said foreign news media bureaus were increasingly being used by spies. Public hand-wringing about spies has been used to justify crackdowns against groups or classes the Kremlin deems as threats, including foreign nongovernmental organizations, whose activities were restricted and more closely regulated with a new law that took effect this year.

2006-12-22.title: Bickering Saudis Struggle for an Answer to Iran’s Rising Influence in the Middle East
2006-12-22.abstract: Debate in Saudi Arabia about Iran's influence in Middle East bursts into open with abrupt resignation of Prince Turki al-Faisal, Saudi ambassador to Washington; resignation is seen by many as part of long-running battle over Saudi Arabia's foreign policy; privately, some Saudi officials and analysts say Prince Turki resigned over deep differences with Prince Bandar bin Sultan over how to deal with Iran; little is known about his successor Adel al-Jubeir, although Saudi officials say Jubeir does not necessarily share Prince Bandar's opinions; photos (M)

2006-12-22.title: On His Ancestors' Wings, a Korean Soars to the U.N.
2006-12-22.abstract: Standing amid snow-covered fields and squat white farmhouses, with fingers chapped from years of planting red peppers and ginseng, Ban Ki-jong proudly traced his village's destiny in the shape of a nearby mountain.     ''See how it looks like a crane,'' he pointed, ''with its wings spread open, ready to fly?''

2006-12-22.title: Marines Charge 4 With Murder of Iraq Civilians
2006-12-22.abstract: The criminal charges are an unusually aggressive judicial reaction to a massacre that has damaged the military’s credibility with Iraqis.

2006-12-22.title: Tensions Flare Over Custody in Rape Case in Philippines
2006-12-22.abstract: Adm William J Fallon, commander of American forces in Pacific, announces that he canceled huge annual military exercise with Philippines in dispute over custody of Marine Lance Cpl Daniel Smith, who was convicted of rape and sentenced in early December; says he will also halt aid and reconstruction programs carried out by Americans in Philippines until he is confident that troops' legal rights will be protected under agreements governing visiting US forces; case has tested relations betwen US and Philippines and sharpened internal political divisions (M)

2006-12-22.title: Names of the Dead
2006-12-22.abstract: Defense Department confirms death of one American service member in Iraq, bringing total to 2,944 (S)

2006-12-23.title: Man Charged With Murders of 5 Prostitutes
2006-12-23.abstract: Steven Wright, charged with murdering five prostitutes whose naked bodies were found over a 10-day period this month, did not enter a plea.

2006-12-23.title: U.N. Postpones Vote on Penalties for Iran
2006-12-23.abstract: The Security Council postponed a vote on a resolution aimed at halting Iran’s nuclear program after Russia objected that the document restricted legitimate business activities.

2006-12-23.title: Search for Climbers in China Gains Focus
2006-12-23.abstract: The decision to focus on two mountains in Sichuan Province came after a tip from a woman who met the pair shortly before they disappeared.

2006-12-23.title: Palestinian Truce Shaky, but Violence Ebbs
2006-12-23.abstract: While there were outbursts of violence, Friday’s death toll was lower than earlier in the week, politicians spoke of calm and the streets were generally quiet.

2006-12-23.title: Military Cutoff By U.S. Draws Filipino Anger
2006-12-23.abstract: US decision to cancel major joint military exercise with Philippines prompts angry denunciations in Manila, with critics accusing Washington of putting pressure on Manila as it seeks to gain custody of Lance Cpl Daniel Smith, marine convicted of raping local woman (M)

2006-12-24.title: Ethiopian Warplanes Attack Somalia
2006-12-24.abstract: With the bombings, Ethiopia openly escalated its conflict with Somalia’s Islamist fighters into a dreaded regional war.

2006-12-24.title: Iraqi Government Officials Reach Out to Shiite Leaders
2006-12-24.abstract: Seeking to avoid possible disintegration, the governing Shiite coalition sent a delegation to visit two of Iraq’s most important unelected figures.

2006-12-24.title: Security Council Approves Sanctions Against Iran Over Nuclear Program
2006-12-24.abstract: United Nations Security Council approves sanctions resolution prepared by Germany and Council's five permanent members that bans Iranian import and export of materials and technology used in uranium enrichment or reprocessing and ballistic missiles; resolution mandates compliance but restricts punishment to non-military methods; resolution was softened repeatedly to placate Russia, which has strong economic ties with Iran (M)

2006-12-24.title: Iran Vows to Continue Nuclear Program
2006-12-24.abstract: Iran also said it would reconsider its relations with the international nuclear monitoring agency.

2006-12-24.title: Hamas Fears Crackdown in Abbas’s Call for Election
2006-12-24.abstract: Hamas sees President Mahmoud Abbas’s demand for early legislative elections as another effort by his Fatah faction to humiliate Hamas and overthrow it.

2006-12-25.title: A Widening Gap Erodes Argentina’s Egalitarian Image
2006-12-25.abstract: Argentina has largely recovered from its economic collapse in 2002; its economy has grown faster than any other in South America since 2003, expanding on average by more than 8 percent annually; another problem has come with revival; fruits of rapid expansion are not being shared by all, and as result, economic and social inequality have intensified; photos (M)

2006-12-25.title: Lawsuit Reopens Old Wounds in German-Polish Dispute
2006-12-25.abstract: Lawsuit by obscure German claims group has prompted Poland to call into question treaty meant to settle forever borders between countries; Polish Foreign Min Anna Fotyga raised doubts about treaty week after group representing Germans expelled from present-day Poland after World War II filed suit at European Court of Human Rights, seeking restitution of their property (M)

2006-12-25.title: At Axis of Episcopal Split, an Anti-Gay Nigerian
2006-12-25.abstract: Archbishop Peter J Akinola, conservative leader of Nigeria's Anglican Church, has emerged at center of schism over homosexuality in global Anglican Communion; Akinola, whose international reputation has been built on his tough stance against homosexuality, has become spiritual head of 21 conservative churches in US; they opted to leave Episcopal Church over its decision to consecrate gay bishop and allow churches to bless same-sex unions; Akinola has created offshoot of his Nigerian church in North America for discontented Americans; he has made himself kingpin of alliance between theological conservatives in North America and developing world that could tip power to conservatives in Anglican Communion; photos (M)

2006-12-25.title: Lawsuit Reopens Old Wounds in German-Polish Dispute
2006-12-25.abstract: A lawsuit by an obscure German claims group has prompted Poland to call into question a treaty meant to forever settle the borders between the countries.

2006-12-25.title: Iran Is Defiant, Vowing to U.N. It Will Continue Nuclear Efforts
2006-12-25.abstract: Iran reacts defiantly to UN Security Council resolution imposing sanctions because of country's nuclear program; Iranian officials vow to continue efforts to enrich uranium and warn that Security Council action will change Iran's relationship with UN's nuclear monitoring agency (M)

2006-12-26.title: Israel to Build New Settlement in Occupied West Bank
2006-12-26.abstract: For the first time in 10 years, Israel said it will build a new Jewish settlement in the occupied West Bank, prompting Palestinian anger and American concern.

2006-12-26.title: Russian Groups Look to Germany for Help
2006-12-26.abstract: News analysis discusses several challenges Germany faces when it takes over European Union presidency and leadership of Group of 8 on Jan 1, 2007; Germany has strongest ties to Russia of any nation in Europe; will be dealing with Russian human rights issues and overall European-Russian relations (M)

2006-12-26.title: British Soldiers Storm Iraqi Jail, Citing Torture
2006-12-26.abstract: Soldiers rescued 127 prisoners from what the British said was almost certain execution and ultimately reducing the facility to rubble.

2006-12-26.title: Tehran Assails U.S. Arrests of Iranians in Iraq Raids
2006-12-26.abstract: Iran's Foreign Ministry condemns arrests of several Iranians by US forces in Iraq; warns arrests could damage relations (S)

2006-12-26.title: Court Upholds Death Penalty for Hussein
2006-12-26.abstract: The decision by an Iraqi appeals court clears the way for Saddam Hussein’s execution within 30 days.

2006-12-27.title: Turkmenistan Limits Election to Soviet-Style Slate
2006-12-27.abstract: Turkmenistan legislature agrees to schedule an election for Feb 11 to choose successor to late Pres Saparmurat Niyazov, but limits those who can run to six candidates of its choice; signals tacit support of acting president, Gurbanguly Berdymukhammedov, by unanimously approving his candidacy; analysts say Soviet-style process of nominating candidates is evidence that country is unlikely to change; photo (M)

2006-12-27.title: 2 More Die as Bird Flu Continues Spreading to Humans in Egypt
2006-12-27.abstract: Egyptian health authorities say several cases of avian flu have spread from poultry to humans in Nile Delta; confirms nine human deaths from avian flu since it was first found in birds in February 2006 (M)

2006-12-27.title: Quake Hits Taiwan; No Tsunami in Area
2006-12-27.abstract: A strong earthquake shook Taiwan, followed by three powerful aftershocks, but the quake did not produce a tsunami.

2006-12-27.title: Iran Is Seeking More Influence in Afghanistan
2006-12-27.abstract: Iran is investing in Afghanistan as part of a bigger drive to spread its influence and ideas farther across the Middle East.

2006-12-27.title: Islamists Retreat in Somalia
2006-12-27.abstract: Islamist forces, once in control of much of Somalia, are crumbling faster than expected from Ethiopian forces and are retreating back to their stronghold in Somalia's battle-scarred capital of Mogadishu; transitional government's troops, with Ethiopian backing, call for Islamists to surrender; Meles Zenawi, Ethiopia's prime minister, says that his soldiers and their Somali allies have 'broken the back' of Islamists; UN officials say thousands of fighters from Eritrea, Yemen, Syria, and Libya have streamed into Somalia to wage holy war against Ethiopia; photos (M)

2006-12-28.title: Israel Says It Will Retaliate Against Rockets From Gaza
2006-12-28.abstract: Israel ordered the military to resume attacks on Palestinians firing rockets from Gaza, more than a month after a cease-fire was declared.

2006-12-28.title: Iran to ‘Revise’ Any Relations With Monitors in Nuclear Area
2006-12-28.abstract: Iran’s parliament overwhelmingly passed a bill that could limit its cooperation with the International Atomic Energy Agency.

2006-12-28.title: Malnutrition Is Cheating Its Survivors, and Africa’s Future
2006-12-28.abstract: Most of Ethiopia’s malnourished children do not die. Instead, they grow up stunted and sickly, weaklings in a land that runs on manual labor.

2006-12-28.title: Sectarian Ties Weaken Duty’s Call for Iraq Forces
2006-12-28.abstract: U.S. forces are struggling with the task of trying to build up Iraqi security forces that are being used as proxies in a spreading sectarian war.

2006-12-28.title: Iran’s Strong Ties With Syria Complicate U.S. Overtures
2006-12-28.abstract: Pressure grows for US to re-engage Syria as part of Middle East strategy; analysts say Syria, with government more pragmatic than ideological, can be pried away from Iran and convinced its long-term interests lie with West; US has isolated Syria for years, while Iran has courted it; US may not be able to cut Syria's wide, deep and longstanding links with Iran; photo (M)

2006-12-29.title: 19 Are Killed as Drug Gangs Conduct Attacks in Brazil
2006-12-29.abstract: Brazilian news organizations said the gang attacks are meant as a warning to the new governor of the state of Rio de Janeiro, Sérgio Cabral.

2006-12-29.title: U.S. Is Being Told Hussein Hanging Seems Imminent
2006-12-29.abstract: American and Iraqi officials suggest that Saddam Hussein could be hanged within days rather than weeks; Iraq's Constitution requires that Iraqi president and two deputies sign all execution orders, but government official familiar with process says little objection would be raised if execution takes place almost immediately; national security adviser Mowaffak al-Rubaie says there would be no advance notice of execution because of fears that any announcement could set off violence; says news media will not be invited to attend hanging; says execution would be videotaped but he doubts that tape would be released; American officials are referring all formal queries on matter to government of Iraq (M)

2006-12-29.title: Names of the Dead
2006-12-29.abstract: Defense Dept confirms deaths of nine service members in Iraq, bringing total to 2,976 (S)

2006-12-29.title: World Briefing | Asia: China: Searchers Identify Body As U.S. Climber
2006-12-29.abstract: Body of Charlie Fowler, one of two elite American climbers, is found high on remote mountain in Sichuan Province, China; he and Christine Boskoff have been missing for more than month; Boskoff has not been found yet; photo (S)

2006-12-29.title: Crisis in Housing Adds to Miseries of Iraq Mayhem
2006-12-29.abstract: Iraq is in midst of housing crisis that worsens by the day; families have been forced out by rising rents and violence; many Iraqis have migrated to squalid squatter encampments as chaos grows and ranks of jobless swell; crisis appears more pronounced among Shiites than Sunnis because of years of economic marginalization they endured under Saddam Hussein; many Iraqi families who do have housing are now living with three or even four generations under one roof; photos (M)

2006-12-30.title: Looking Back at World Events
2006-12-30.abstract: International Herald Tribune managing editor Alison Smale looks back on the events that made international news in 2006.

2006-12-30.title: Somalis Split as Fighting Halts and Hint of Insurgency Looms
2006-12-30.abstract: Anti-Ethiopia riots erupt in Mogadishu day after Ethiopian-backed troops capture capital of Somalia from Islamist forces; masked gunmen emerge for first time on streets in what may be sign of developing insurgency (M)

2006-12-30.title: Dictator Who Ruled Iraq With Violence Is Hanged for Crimes Against Humanity
2006-12-30.abstract: Saddam Hussein’s execution came with terrible swiftness after he lost the appeal of his death sentence.

2006-12-30.title: Struggling to Save His Amazon, From the Top of a Death List
2006-12-30.abstract: The entity Tarcísio Feitosa da Silva leads has challenged forged land titles, denounced unauthorized logging and organized peasant farmers to resist land invasions.

2006-12-30.title: 4 Suspects in ’94 Massacres of Rwanda Tutsi Are Held in London to Face Extradition
2006-12-30.abstract: The men were arrested under an unusual and previously secret memorandum of understanding between Britain and Rwanda.

2006-12-31.title: Hussein’s Case Won’t Bolster International Human Rights Law, Experts Fear
2006-12-31.abstract: Saddam Hussein is one of few modern leaders to be tried and executed for his crimes and other abuses of power, but his case is distinguished from those of other former leaders in past decade; Hussein is first former leader to be tried by domestic court for crimes against humanity, unusual application of international law; legal experts say Hussein case has not significantly reinforced efforts to apply concepts of international human rights law around the world; say case was widely viewed as flawed and politicized; rights groups and international lawyers have deplored execution as hasty and based on comparatively small case, precluding second trial on weightier genocide charge; credibility of Baghdad tribunal was called into question because it was organized by US and because of death sentence; new international legal institutions like International Criminal Court do not impose death penalty (M)

2006-12-31.title: Somalia’s Islamists Vow Never to Surrender
2006-12-31.abstract: Ethiopian troops were preparing to seize Kismayo, a port city, possibly setting the stage for a major battle.

2006-12-31.title: Around the World, Unease and Criticism of Penalty
2006-12-31.abstract: Western politicians sought a balance between revulsion at Saddam Hussein’s record, support for his executioners and concern at the use of a capital penalty they largely shun in their own countries.

2006-12-31.title: Hussein Is Buried in His Hometown
2006-12-31.abstract: Like much else about Saddam Hussein’s life and death, his passage back to the town where he grew up, Awja, Iraq, was marked by bitterness and dispute.

2006-12-31.title: Around the World, Unease and Criticism of Penalty
2006-12-31.abstract: Western politicians seek uneasy balance between revulsion at Saddam Hussein's record of human rights violations, support for his executioners and concern at use of capital punishment they largely shun in their own countries; European Union reiterates its opposition to death penalty; Russia expresses regret that Iraqi officials rejected international appeals not to carry out execution; Israel reacts calmly; Christian and Muslim religious leaders are more critical in their condemnation of Hussein's execution; photo (M)
